title: Quantum Erasure Imaging: Complementary Modalities from Delayed-Choice Erasure
abstract: Quantum Erasure Imaging (QEI) turns delayed-choice erasure into a practical imaging protocol. Entangled photon pairs encode two classical modalities, absorption $T(x,y)$ and a phase-sensitive cosine quadrature of $φ(x,y)$, reconstructed from a single run of time-tagged coincidences by retrospective sorting on a remote ancilla. Measuring the ancilla in H/V yields $T$ via which-path information; D/A yields interference visibility $\propto \frac{2\sqrt{T}}{T+1}\cosφ$; and a rotated orthonormal analyzer continuously trades between them. We derive balanced two-port estimators whose denominators are analyzer independent (completeness / no signaling), together with Fisher information (FI) and Cramér--Rao bounds (CRBs) that establish an equivalence to time division under labeled randomization. The advantages of QEI are operational: single-run acquisition, perfect co-registration, and remote / delayed mode choice. We illustrate the protocol with Monte-Carlo simulations and open source our code.
summary: This paper introduces Quantum Erasure Imaging (QEI), a novel protocol that uses polarization-entangled photons to simultaneously measure two complementary properties of an object: absorption and phase. By analyzing coincidence counts from a single run, the authors derive robust estimators and prove that the information gained is theoretically equivalent to time-division measurements, offering significant operational advantages for quantum imaging.
main_problem: To develop Quantum Erasure Imaging (QEI) as a practical protocol to reconstruct two complementary classical modalities—absorption $T(x, y)$ and phase $\phi(x, y)$—from a single run of time-tagged coincidence measurements.
main_result: QEI enables single-run acquisition of co-registered complementary observables, and its information-theoretic performance (Fisher Information) is shown to be equivalent to time-division measurements under labeled randomization.
method: The authors derive balanced two-port estimators and calculate the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) and Cramér-Rao Bounds (CRBs) based on coincidence counting statistics from polarization-entangled photon pairs.
model_or_system: The system uses polarization-entangled photon pairs generated via Type-II SPDC. The imaging process involves the imaging photon passing through an object imparting $T(x, y)$ and $\phi(x, y)$, while the ancilla photon measurement dictates the reconstruction modality.
key_observables: Absorption $T(x, y)$, phase $\phi(x, y)$, interference visibility $V(x, y)$, and the generalized visibility $V_	heta(x, y)$.
important_parameters: The analyzer angle $	heta$ which continuously trades between path information and interference contrast.
main_assumptions: The FIM equivalence proof assumes equal detection efficiencies across ancilla outcomes and negligible system drift.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the QEI setup; other figures show the estimation of the phase $\phi$ using specific analyzer angles and quantify the performance using the FIM and CRBs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces QEI, details the reconstruction formulas for $T$ and $\phi$ based on different ancilla measurements (H/V vs D/A), derives the robust estimators, calculates the FIM, and finally establishes the operational advantages by comparing it to time-division methods.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems, providing a concrete, advanced protocol for quantum state tomography and imaging that leverages entanglement and delayed choice principles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. QEI schematic. The imaging photon A traverses a modified Mach–Zehnder: PBS1 encodes which–path on polariza- tion; the object in Arm 1 imparts T(x, y), ϕ(x, y); a HWP equalizes polarization; and BS2 recombines to two ports (D1,D2). The ancilla photon B is measured in a random/delayed analyzer (H/V, D/A, or a rotated orthonormal basis {|e±(θ)⟩}). All events are time–tagged and retrospectively sorted by B’s outcome to reconstruct co–registered absorption and phase images from a single run. Two–port normalization makes the denominator analyzer–independent (completeness/no–signaling).

FIG. 2. Illustration of the reconstruction of T(x, y) using H/V polarization bases. (a) Image representing True T(x, y). (b) A vortex phase map associated with the sample. Measur- ing B: (c) Reconstructed T(x, y). (d) Estimated uncertainty in the reconstructed T(x, y).

FIG. 3. Measuring B on H/V bases. (a) T(x, y) Distribu- tion: brown and blue histograms, respectively, represent the true T(x, y) and reconstructed values of T(x, y). (b) Correla- tion coefficient between the true T and reconstructed T at a simulated 1000 photons per pixel.

FIG. 4. Measuring B on D/A bases: (a) Two-port normalized differential corresponding to the given image T(x, y). (b) Es- timated uncertainty in the visibility derived from coincidence counting statistics. Measuring B on rotational |e±(θ)⟩bases: (c) Visibility evaluated at θ = π/8. (d) Reconstruction of phase ϕ (modulo π) based on the visibility shown in panel (c).
